members.name: AutoConfig

methods.name: from_pretrained
methods.summary: Return the `ModelConfig` for `name_or_path`.

parameters.name: name_or_path
parameters.description:
Registered model name (e.g. `"vit_base_16"`) or a directory
saved by `PretrainedModel.save_pretrained`.

returns.description:
The default configuration dataclass for that family (no
weights are downloaded or instantiated).

raises.description: If the name is unknown to the registry.
If a directory path is supplied but `config.json` is absent.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoConfig
>>> cfg = AutoConfig.from_pretrained("bert_base")
>>> cfg.hidden_size
768
notes:
Resolution order:

1. `default_config` fast path — O(1) when the registry entry
   pre-declared one at `@register_model` time.
2. Factory fallback — invokes the factory with `pretrained=False`
   and reads `.config` off the resulting model.  Still allocates
   parameters, but no checkpoint download.

members.summary: Generic config loader — return the `ModelConfig` for any name.
members.extended:
Use this when you only need the hyper-parameter dataclass for a
registered model, without paying the cost of allocating its weights.
Typical applications: introspecting layer widths, building parity
tests, or feeding the config into a custom training loop.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoConfig
>>> cfg = AutoConfig.from_pretrained("resnet_50")
>>> cfg.model_type
'resnet'
>>> cfg.num_classes
1000
notes:
`AutoConfig` is not instantiable — call
`AutoConfig.from_pretrained` directly.  Resolution has two
fast paths:

1. If the registry entry carries a `default_config` (recommended
   for all Phase 1+ registrations), that config object is returned
   immediately — zero model construction.
2. Otherwise, the factory is invoked with `pretrained=False` and the
   returned model's `.config` is read; no checkpoint is downloaded.

Local directories are also accepted: `config.json` is parsed and a
matching `ModelConfig` subclass is reconstructed via
`ModelConfig.from_dict`.

members.name: AutoModel
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads the *backbone* of any family.
members.extended:
"Backbone" means the family base class with no task head attached
(e.g. `ResNet`, not `ResNetForImageClassification`).  Use this
when you want raw feature outputs or intend to attach a custom head
on top.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModel
>>> backbone = AutoModel.from_pretrained("resnet_50")
>>> type(backbone).__name__
'ResNet'
>>> import lucid
>>> features = backbone.forward_features(lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224))
>>> features.shape
(1, 2048, 7, 7)
notes:
Each registered model carries a `_task` tag; this class filters on
`_task == "base"`.  A given name can resolve to different classes
under different `AutoModelFor*` shells — for example,
`AutoModel.from_pretrained("resnet_50")` returns `ResNet` whereas
`AutoModelForImageClassification.from_pretrained("resnet_50")`
returns `ResNetForImageClassification`.

members.name: AutoModelForCausalLM
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a causal (left-to-right) LM.
members.extended:
Resolves names like `"gpt"` / `"gpt2_small"` to their concrete
`{Family}LMHeadModel` subclass.  The model is suitable for
next-token prediction and autoregressive generation via
`GenerationMixin.generate`.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForCausalLM
>>> model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt")
>>> type(model).__name__
'GPTLMHeadModel'
notes:
The forward returns a `CausalLMOutput` with `logits` shaped
`(B, T, vocab_size)` and optionally a `past_key_values` tuple for
cache-friendly decoding.  Models tagged `"causal-lm"` typically
expose `config.vocab_size` / `pad_token_id` / `bos_token_id` /
`eos_token_id`.

members.name: AutoModelForImageClassification
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a registered image classifier.
members.extended:
The Auto* family lets you write framework-agnostic code that picks
the right model at runtime — given a model name string, Lucid looks
up the registry, verifies the model's declared task matches, and
returns the concrete subclass with weights loaded.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForImageClassification
>>> model = AutoModelForImageClassification.from_pretrained("resnet_50")
>>> type(model).__name__
'ResNetForImageClassification'
>>> import lucid
>>> out = model(lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224))
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Each registered model declares a class-level
`_task: ClassVar[str]`.  This Auto class restricts lookups to
entries tagged `"image-classification"` and raises
`ValueError` on mismatch — preventing accidental loads of a
detection backbone or a language model as a classifier.

The returned object is a concrete subclass such as
`ResNetForImageClassification` or `ViTForImageClassification`;
its `forward(images)` returns an `ImageClassificationOutput`
with `logits` of shape `(B, num_classes)`.

members.name: AutoModelForImageGeneration
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads an image-generation model.
members.extended:
Resolves names like `"vae_gen"` / `"ddpm_cifar_gen"` /
`"ncsn_cifar_gen"` to their concrete `{Family}ForImageGeneration`
subclass — VAE / DDPM / NCSN today, future flow models tomorrow.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForImageGeneration
>>> model = AutoModelForImageGeneration.from_pretrained("ddpm_cifar_gen")
>>> type(model).__name__
'DDPMForImageGeneration'
notes:
All `ForImageGeneration` subclasses expose a `generate(...)`
method (via `DiffusionMixin` for diffusion models, or a
family-specific method for VAEs).  See
`GenerationOutput` for the structure of the returned samples.

members.name: AutoModelForMaskedLM
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a masked-LM model.
members.extended:
Resolves names like `"bert_base_mlm"` / `"roformer_mlm"` to their
concrete `{Family}ForMaskedLM` subclass — bidirectional models
trained with the masked-token reconstruction objective.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForMaskedLM
>>> model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("bert_base_mlm")
>>> type(model).__name__
'BERTForMaskedLM'
notes:
The forward returns a `MaskedLMOutput` with `logits` of shape
`(B, T, vocab_size)`.  Subclasses use
`MaskedLMMixin.compute_lm_loss` to reduce against `(B, T)`
label tensors with `ignore_index=-100` for non-masked positions.

members.name: AutoModelForObjectDetection
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a registered object detector.
members.extended:
Resolves names like `"faster_rcnn"` / `"detr_resnet50"` /
`"yolo_v3"` to their concrete `{Family}ForObjectDetection`
subclass.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForObjectDetection
>>> model = AutoModelForObjectDetection.from_pretrained("detr_resnet50")
>>> type(model).__name__
'DETRForObjectDetection'
notes:
The detector forward returns an `ObjectDetectionOutput` with
`logits` (class scores per proposal / query) and `pred_boxes`
(predicted box coordinates).  Two-stage detectors additionally
populate `proposals` so postprocessing can be reconstructed from
the output alone.

members.name: AutoModelForQuestionAnswering
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a SQuAD-style QA head.
members.extended:
Resolves to `{Family}ForQuestionAnswering` across BERT / RoFormer —
models that emit per-token start / end logits over the input sequence.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForQuestionAnswering
>>> model = AutoModelForQuestionAnswering.from_pretrained("bert_base_qa")
>>> type(model).__name__
'BERTForQuestionAnswering'
notes:
The forward returns an output with `start_logits` and `end_logits`
of shape `(B, T)` each.  Inference picks the (start, end) span that
maximises `start_logits[s] + end_logits[e]` subject to `s <= e`.

members.name: AutoModelForSemanticSegmentation
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a semantic-segmentation model.
members.extended:
Resolves names like `"fcn_resnet50"` / `"unet"` /
`"maskformer_resnet50"` to their concrete
`{Family}ForSemanticSegmentation` subclass.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForSemanticSegmentation
>>> model = AutoModelForSemanticSegmentation.from_pretrained("fcn_resnet50")
>>> type(model).__name__
'FCNForSemanticSegmentation'
notes:
The forward returns a `SemanticSegmentationOutput` whose
`logits` carry per-pixel class scores of shape
`(B, num_classes, H, W)`.

members.name: AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a seq2seq (encoder-decoder) model.
members.extended:
Covers translation / summarisation heads — currently the Vaswani
Transformer family (`transformer_base_seq2seq` /
`transformer_large_seq2seq`).  T5, BART, and mBART are the natural
future consumers of this Auto class.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM
>>> model = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained("transformer_base_seq2seq")
>>> type(model).__name__
'TransformerForSeq2SeqLM'
notes:
The forward returns a `Seq2SeqLMOutput` carrying decoder
`logits`, optional decoder hidden states / attentions, and the
encoder's outputs so callers can cache them across multiple decoder
passes.

members.name: AutoModelForSequenceClassification
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a sentence-level classifier.
members.extended:
Resolves to `{Family}ForSequenceClassification` for GLUE / sentiment
/ NLI fine-tunes across BERT / GPT / GPT-2 / RoFormer / Transformer.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForSequenceClassification
>>> model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("bert_base_cls")
>>> type(model).__name__
'BERTForSequenceClassification'
notes:
The forward returns an output whose `logits` are shaped
`(B, num_labels)`.  Each backbone wraps a pooled representation
(CLS for BERT-style, last non-pad token for GPT-style) through a
Linear classifier head.

members.name: AutoModelForTokenClassification
members.summary: Auto-dispatching wrapper that loads a per-token classifier.
members.extended:
Resolves to `{Family}ForTokenClassification` for NER / POS-tagging
fine-tunes across BERT / RoFormer / Transformer.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import AutoModelForTokenClassification
>>> model = AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained("bert_base_token_cls")
>>> type(model).__name__
'BERTForTokenClassification'
notes:
The forward returns `logits` of shape `(B, T, num_labels)` and is
typically trained against `(B, T)` label tensors via
`MaskedLMMixin.compute_lm_loss`, which folds the sequence axis
into the batch axis for cross-entropy.

members.name: ModelConfig

methods.name: to_dict
methods.summary: Serialise the config (including `model_type`) to a plain dict.

returns.description:
JSON-friendly mapping of every dataclass field plus the
`model_type` ClassVar.

raises.description: If `self` is not a `@dataclass`.

examples:
>>> cfg = MyConfig(hidden_size=1024)
>>> d = cfg.to_dict()
>>> d["hidden_size"], d["model_type"]
(1024, 'myfamily')

methods.name: from_dict
methods.summary: Reconstruct a config from a plain dict (inverse of `to_dict`).

parameters.name: d
parameters.description:
Field-name → value mapping; `model_type` is silently
stripped (it's a ClassVar, not a constructor arg).

raises.description: If `cls` is not a `@dataclass`.

examples:
>>> cfg = MyConfig.from_dict({"hidden_size": 512, "model_type": "myfamily"})
>>> cfg.hidden_size
512

warns.description:
When `d` contains keys not declared on `cls` — those keys
are dropped before invoking the constructor so older code can
load newer checkpoints with added fields.

methods.name: save
methods.summary: Write the config as pretty-printed JSON to `path`.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to write.  Existing files are overwritten.

examples:
>>> cfg = MyConfig(hidden_size=1024)
>>> cfg.save("/tmp/myconfig.json")

methods.name: load
methods.summary: Load a config from a JSON file (inverse of `save`).

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: Path to a JSON file produced by `save`.

raises.description: If `path` does not contain a JSON object.

examples: >>> cfg = MyConfig.load("/tmp/myconfig.json")

members.summary: Base class for every model's configuration dataclass.
members.extended:
A `ModelConfig` subclass holds the hyper-parameters that fully
determine a model's architecture (depths, widths, vocab sizes,
activation choices, …).  Configs are immutable, JSON-serialisable,
and the single argument every concrete `PretrainedModel`
constructor accepts.

Subclasses must:

- Use `@dataclass(frozen=True)` (immutability + `fields()`
  introspection).
- Set the `model_type` `ClassVar[str]` to a unique family
  identifier (e.g. `"resnet"`, `"bert"`).
- Optionally implement `__post_init__` for cross-field validation.
examples:
>>> from dataclasses import dataclass
>>> @dataclass(frozen=True)
... class MyConfig(ModelConfig):
...     model_type: ClassVar[str] = "myfamily"
...     hidden_size: int = 768
...     num_layers: int = 12
>>> cfg = MyConfig(hidden_size=1024)
>>> cfg.to_dict()["model_type"]
'myfamily'
notes:
The base class provides a JSON round-trip via `to_dict` /
`from_dict` / `save` / `load`.  Unknown fields are
tolerated on load with a `UserWarning` — this lets newer
checkpoints (which may have added fields) load into older code
without crashing.

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description:
Persistent family identifier.  Embedded in `config.json` on
disk and used by directory-based loading to find the correct
registry entry.

see_also.name: Class decorator that attaches paper / theory / display metadata
subclass.  Concrete families wrap their Config with this so the
docs site can render family-root index pages.

members.name: PretrainedModel

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the module and validate the supplied config.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Must be an instance of the subclass's declared
`config_class`.

raises.description:
If `config_class` has not been set on the concrete subclass,
or if `config` is not an instance of `config_class`.

methods.name: from_pretrained
methods.summary: Load a model from a registered name or a local directory.
methods.extended:
Two modes are supported:

1. **Registered name** (`"resnet_50"` / `"resnet-50"`) —
   looked up in the global registry; the factory is invoked with
   `pretrained=True`.  The factory result is validated to be an
   instance of `cls` (so subclass calls like
   `ResNet.from_pretrained("vit_base_16")` raise rather than
   silently returning the wrong family).
2. **Local directory** containing `config.json` plus either
   `model.safetensors` (preferred) or `weights.lucid`.  The
   config is restored via `cls.config_class.load` and the
   weights are loaded with `lucid.load`.

parameters.name: name_or_path
parameters.description: Registered model name or filesystem directory path.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description: Forwarded to `load_state_dict`.

raises.description: If the name is unrecognised and is not a valid directory.
If a directory was supplied but required files are absent.

If the registered factory yields a non-subclass of `cls`,
or `config_class` is unset, or the weights file lacks a
state-dict.

examples:
>>> # Registered name
>>> model = ResNetForImageClassification.from_pretrained("resnet_50")
>>>
>>> # Local directory
>>> model.save_pretrained("/tmp/my_resnet50")
>>> reloaded = ResNetForImageClassification.from_pretrained("/tmp/my_resnet50")
notes:
For task-aware dispatch that resolves to a different concrete
subclass per task, use the `AutoModelFor*` family instead.

methods.name: save_pretrained
methods.summary: Write `config.json` and weights to `path`.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: Destination directory.  Created if it does not exist.

parameters.name: safe_serialization
parameters.description:
If `True`, save weights as `model.safetensors` (requires
`pip install safetensors`).  If `False`, save as
`weights.lucid` using the native pickle-based format.

examples:
>>> model = create_model("resnet_50")
>>> model.save_pretrained("/tmp/my_resnet50", safe_serialization=True)
notes:
Output layout:

    path/
      config.json
      model.safetensors   # when safe_serialization=True
      weights.lucid       # when safe_serialization=False

The companion `from_pretrained` (or any `AutoModelFor*`
class) reads this directory layout and prefers SafeTensors when
both files are present.

methods.name: num_parameters
methods.summary: Return the total number of elements across all parameters.

parameters.name: only_trainable
parameters.description:
When `True`, parameters with `requires_grad=False` are
excluded (useful for reporting trainable model size after
freezing the backbone).

returns.description: Sum of `prod(p.shape)` over the selected parameters.

examples:
>>> model = create_model("resnet_50")
>>> model.num_parameters()
25557032
>>> for p in model.backbone.parameters():
...     p.requires_grad = False
>>> model.num_parameters(only_trainable=True) < 25557032
True

methods.name: get_input_embeddings
methods.summary: Return the input-embedding submodule, or `None` if not applicable.

returns.description:
The embedding layer for text / token-id models (BERT, GPT,
…); `None` for vision and other non-embedding models.

notes:
Override in subclasses that have an embedding table.  Used by
tools that need to resize / share / introspect token embeddings
without coupling to family-specific attribute names.

methods.name: set_input_embeddings
methods.summary: Replace the input-embedding submodule.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: New embedding module.

raises.description: On the base class — subclasses with embeddings must override.

notes:
Companion to `get_input_embeddings`.  Subclasses that
override one should override the other.

members.summary: Base for every Lucid model that supports the pretrained-checkpoint flow.
members.extended:
Subclasses inherit `from_pretrained` / `save_pretrained`
plus parameter-counting and embedding-access helpers.  The contract:

- Set `config_class = MyConfig` as a `ClassVar` — required;
  enforced at `__init__` time.
- Define `__init__(self, config: MyConfig) -> None` taking a single
  config argument.  All architectural variation (depth, width,
  activation choices, …) belongs inside the config — no extra
  constructor parameters.
- Implement `forward(...) -> ModelOutput` returning one of the
  dataclasses in `lucid.models._output`.
examples:
>>> class MyModel(PretrainedModel):
...     config_class: ClassVar[type[MyConfig]] = MyConfig
...     def __init__(self, config):
...         super().__init__(config)
...         self.linear = nn.Linear(config.hidden_size, config.num_classes)
...     def forward(self, x):
...         return self.linear(x)
>>> model = MyModel(MyConfig(hidden_size=128, num_classes=10))
>>> model.num_parameters()
1290
notes:
The class is a thin lift on `lucid.nn.Module` — all parameter
management still flows through the standard `Module` machinery.
Two persistence formats are supported when saving / loading: the
pickle-based `weights.lucid` (default) and the cross-framework
`model.safetensors`.

attributes.name: config_class
attributes.description:
Subclasses MUST override.  `None` signals "not set" — the base
`__init__` raises in that case.

attributes.name: base_model_prefix
attributes.description:
Optional name of the backbone attribute (e.g. `"bert"` on
`BERTForMaskedLM`).  Used by future state-dict remapping logic
when loading head-less checkpoints into head-bearing models.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Instance attribute populated by `__init__`.

members.name: BackboneMixin

methods.name: forward_features
methods.summary: Run the backbone's feature-extraction pass.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input image batch, shape `(B, C, H, W)`.

returns.description:
Feature map at the deepest stage.  Concrete subclasses may
additionally stash earlier stages on the instance so
multi-stage consumers can fetch them.

methods.name: feature_info
methods.summary: Return the per-stage feature pyramid specification.

members.summary: Marker mixin for models usable as feature extractors.
members.extended:
Detection and segmentation heads consume backbones through this small
protocol: a `forward_features(x)` that emits the deepest stage's
feature map, and a `feature_info` property enumerating every
emitted stage's spec.  Implementing classes get a uniform interface
that head builders (FPN, BiFPN, …) can target.
examples:
>>> class MyBackbone(PretrainedModel, BackboneMixin):
...     def forward_features(self, x):
...         return self.stages(x)
...     @property
...     def feature_info(self):
...         return [FeatureInfo(0, 64, 4), FeatureInfo(1, 128, 8)]
notes:
The mixin carries no state — it only declares two abstract slots.
Subclasses MUST override:

* `forward_features` — returns the final stage's feature map
  (and may stash intermediate stages on `self` for downstream
  use).
* `feature_info` — returns a `list[FeatureInfo]` with one
  entry per stage the backbone exposes.

members.name: ClassificationHeadMixin

methods.name: reset_classifier
methods.summary: Replace the final `Linear` with a freshly initialised one.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
New output dimensionality.  `in_features` is preserved from
the current head.

raises.description: If the head is a `Sequential` containing no `Linear`.
If the head is some other custom module type.

examples:
>>> model = AutoModelForImageClassification.from_pretrained("resnet_50")
>>> model.reset_classifier(num_classes=10)
>>> model(lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)).logits.shape
(1, 10)
notes:
Used during transfer learning — typically called immediately
after `from_pretrained` to adapt a 1000-class ImageNet head
to a downstream task with a different label set.

members.summary: Standard Linear classification head + transfer-learning helper.
members.extended:
Adds two methods to any classifier subclass: `_build_classifier`
constructs a `Linear` (optionally wrapped in a `Dropout`)
according to the config, and `reset_classifier` swaps in a
freshly-initialised Linear with a new `num_classes` for transfer
learning.
examples:
>>> class MyClassifier(PretrainedModel, ClassificationHeadMixin):
...     def __init__(self, config):
...         super().__init__(config)
...         self.backbone = MyBackbone(config)
...         self._build_classifier(config.hidden_size, config.num_classes,
...                                dropout=config.dropout)
>>> model = MyClassifier(cfg)
>>> model.reset_classifier(num_classes=10)
notes:
The contract: subclasses must call `self._build_classifier(...)`
inside their `__init__` *after* `super().__init__(config)` so the
backbone is in place before the head is attached.

The host class exposes `self.classifier` as either a plain
`nn.Linear` (no dropout configured) or an `nn.Sequential` of
`[Dropout, Linear]`; `reset_classifier` handles both shapes.

members.name: DiffusionMixin

methods.name: generate
methods.signature: generate(scheduler: DiffusionScheduler, n_samples: int = 1, num_inference_steps: int | None = None, generator_shape: tuple[int, ...] | None = None, return_intermediates: bool = False, device: str = 'cpu')
methods.summary: Sample `n_samples` images via the reverse diffusion process.

parameters.name: scheduler
parameters.description:
Already-constructed scheduler (DDPM, DDIM, etc.).  Encapsulates
the noise schedule and the per-step update rule.

parameters.name: n_samples
parameters.description: Batch size of the generated output.

parameters.name: num_inference_steps
generator_shape
parameters.description:
Per-sample tensor shape, `(C, H, W)`.  When omitted,
defaults to `(in_channels, H, W)` derived from
`self.config`.

parameters.name: return_intermediates
parameters.description:
If `True`, every per-step sample is recorded and returned
in `GenerationOutput.intermediates`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device to allocate the initial Gaussian noise tensor on.

raises.description:
If `generator_shape` is `None` and the model lacks a
`config` with `sample_size` / `in_channels` fields.

examples:
>>> model = AutoModelForImageGeneration.from_pretrained("ddpm_cifar_gen")
>>> scheduler = DDPMScheduler(num_train_timesteps=1000)
>>> out = model.generate(scheduler, n_samples=4, num_inference_steps=50,
...                      device="cpu")
>>> out.samples.shape
(4, 3, 32, 32)
notes:
Decorated with `lucid.no_grad` — sampling never builds an
autograd graph.  Standard reverse-process loop:

1. Draw $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
2. For each `t` in `scheduler.timesteps` (descending):
   run the model forward, then apply
   `scheduler.step(model_pred, t, x_t)` to obtain $x_{t-1}$.

members.summary: Reverse-process sampling loop for diffusion-family generative models.
members.extended:
Concrete subclasses must:

* Inherit from `lucid.models.PretrainedModel`.
* Define `forward(sample, timestep, ...) -> DiffusionModelOutput`
  that returns the network's prediction at the supplied timestep.
* Expose a `config` attribute carrying `sample_size` /
  `in_channels` (fields `DiffusionModelConfig` defines) — or
  explicitly pass `generator_shape` to `generate`.

The mixin then provides `generate` — a vanilla reverse-process
loop parametrised by an externally-supplied
`DiffusionScheduler`.  Sampler choice (DDPM, DDIM, …) is thus
a runtime knob rather than baked into the model class.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import DDPMScheduler, AutoModelForImageGeneration
>>> model = AutoModelForImageGeneration.from_pretrained("ddpm_cifar_gen")
>>> scheduler = DDPMScheduler(num_train_timesteps=1000)
>>> out = model.generate(scheduler, n_samples=4, num_inference_steps=50)
>>> out.samples.shape
(4, 3, 32, 32)
notes:
The mixin is stateless.  All hyper-parameters (number of inference
steps, image shape, batch size) flow through `generate`'s
keyword arguments; the model class itself stays sampler-agnostic.

members.name: FeatureInfo
members.signature: FeatureInfo(stage: int, num_channels: int, reduction: int)
members.summary: Specification of one stage emitted by a feature-extracting backbone.
members.extended:
Used by detection / segmentation heads that consume a *feature
pyramid*: each entry describes the channel count and spatial
down-sampling factor of one backbone stage relative to the input.
examples:
>>> info = FeatureInfo(stage=3, num_channels=512, reduction=32)
>>> info.reduction
32
notes:
The layout is timm-compatible: detection heads that consume timm
backbones drop in unchanged.  See `BackboneMixin` for the
protocol that produces a `list[FeatureInfo]`.

attributes.name: stage
attributes.description:
Zero-based stage index, in the order the backbone emits features
(typically deepening from stage 0 toward higher receptive field).

attributes.name: num_channels
attributes.description: Channel count of the feature map at this stage.

attributes.name: reduction
attributes.description:
Spatial down-sampling factor vs the network input — e.g. `4`,
`8`, `16`, `32` for the canonical CNN stage taps.

members.name: GenerationMixin

methods.name: generate
methods.signature: generate(input_ids: Tensor, max_length: int = 20, max_new_tokens: int | None = None, do_sample: bool = False, temperature: float = 1.0, top_k: int | None = None, top_p: float | None = None, repetition_penalty: float = 1.0, pad_token_id: int | None = None, eos_token_id: int | None = None)
methods.summary: Autoregressively extend `input_ids` until a stop condition.

parameters.name: input_ids
parameters.description: `(B, T_prompt)` int prompt tokens.

parameters.name: max_length
parameters.description:
Total sequence length cap (prompt + generated).  Ignored when
`max_new_tokens` is supplied.

parameters.name: max_new_tokens
parameters.description:
Cap on the number of *new* tokens to generate, additive over
the prompt length.  Takes precedence over `max_length`.

parameters.name: do_sample
parameters.description:
`False` → greedy argmax decoding; `True` → stochastic
sampling honouring `temperature` / `top_k` / `top_p`.

parameters.name: temperature
parameters.description:
Logit divisor before softmax.  `< 1` sharpens, `> 1`
flattens.  Ignored under greedy decoding.

parameters.name: top_k
parameters.description:
Keep only the K highest-probability tokens before sampling.
`None` disables.

parameters.name: top_p
parameters.description:
Nucleus sampling — keep the smallest token set whose
cumulative probability ≥ `top_p`.  `None` disables.

parameters.name: repetition_penalty
parameters.description:
Multiply the logit of every previously generated token by
`1 / penalty` (and by `penalty` if the logit is
negative).  `1.0` → no effect; `> 1` discourages
repetition.

parameters.name: pad_token_id
parameters.description:
Token id used to pad finished sequences after they emit
`eos_token_id`.  Defaults to `config.pad_token_id`, then
`0` if unset.

parameters.name: eos_token_id
parameters.description:
Stop generating per-sequence once this id is emitted.
Defaults to `config.eos_token_id`.

returns.description:
`(B, T_final)` int tensor where `T_final` ≤ `max_length`
(or `T_prompt + max_new_tokens`).  Sequences that hit
`eos_token_id` early are right-padded with `pad_token_id`.

raises.description: If `input_ids` is not 2-D.

examples:
>>> model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt")
>>> tokens = lucid.tensor([[1, 2, 3]]).long()
>>> out = model.generate(tokens, max_new_tokens=5)
>>> out.shape
(1, 8)
notes:
Decorated with `lucid.no_grad` — generation never builds an
autograd graph.  Each step:

1. Materialise the current prefix as `(B, T_cur)`.
2. Run a forward pass; take logits at the last position.
3. Apply repetition penalty.
4. Greedy argmax, or apply temperature / top-k / top-p and
   inverse-CDF sample one token per row.
5. Replace finished rows' tokens with `pad_token_id`.

Loop breaks early when every row has emitted EOS.

members.summary: Decoder-only autoregressive sampling for causal LM heads.
members.extended:
Provides `generate` — greedy or stochastic next-token sampling
with temperature, top-k, top-p (nucleus), and repetition-penalty
knobs.  Per-sequence stopping at `eos_token_id` is honoured.

Concrete subclasses must:

* Inherit from `lucid.models.PretrainedModel`.
* Define `forward(input_ids, ...)` returning a
  `lucid.models.CausalLMOutput` with `logits` of shape
  `(B, T, vocab_size)`.
* Expose a `config` attribute carrying `vocab_size` /
  `pad_token_id` / `bos_token_id` / `eos_token_id` (fields
  `lucid.models.text.LanguageModelConfig` defines).
examples:
>>> model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2_small")
>>> prompt = lucid.tensor([[1, 2, 3]]).long()
>>> out = model.generate(prompt, max_new_tokens=10, do_sample=True, top_p=0.9)
>>> out.shape
(1, 13)
notes:
The first implementation does **not** use the model's KV cache —
every step re-runs the full prefix.  This is correct but O(T²) in
the prefix length.  Cache support is planned once GPT-2 lands and a
concrete `past_key_values` shape is available to plumb through.

members.name: MaskedLMMixin

methods.name: compute_lm_loss
methods.signature: compute_lm_loss(logits: Tensor, labels: Tensor, ignore_index: int = -100)
methods.summary: Compute per-token cross-entropy loss for an LM head.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description: `(B, T, V)` per-token logits.

parameters.name: labels
parameters.description:
`(B, T)` int target token ids.  Entries equal to
`ignore_index` contribute zero loss and zero gradient.

parameters.name: ignore_index
parameters.description:
Token id to skip during reduction.  Convention from the wider
ML ecosystem: `-100` marks non-masked positions in MLM and
padding tokens in token classification.

returns.description: Scalar loss tensor (mean over the contributing positions).

examples:
>>> logits = lucid.randn(2, 10, 32000)
>>> labels = lucid.randint(0, 32000, (2, 10))
>>> loss = MaskedLMMixin.compute_lm_loss(logits, labels)
>>> loss.shape
()
notes:
Implementation: flatten `logits` to `(B*T, V)` and `labels`
to `(B*T,)`, cast labels to `long`, then call
`lucid.nn.functional.cross_entropy`.  No probabilistic
reweighting; the loss is a plain mean over non-ignored positions.

members.summary: Per-token cross-entropy loss helper for masked-LM / token-class heads.
members.extended:
Encoder text families (BERT, RoFormer, …) all reduce a `(B, T, V)`
logit tensor against `(B, T)` labels with the same recipe: flatten
the sequence axis into the batch axis, then run
`lucid.nn.functional.cross_entropy` with `ignore_index=-100`.
Centralising the call here removes a 3-line stanza repeated across
every `ForMaskedLM` / `ForTokenClassification` class.
examples:
>>> class BERTForMaskedLM(PretrainedModel, MaskedLMMixin):
...     def forward(self, input_ids, labels=None):
...         logits = self.head(self.bert(input_ids))
...         loss = self.compute_lm_loss(logits, labels) if labels else None
...         return MaskedLMOutput(logits=logits, loss=loss)
notes:
The mixin is stateless and exposes a single `@staticmethod` —
inheriting from it is purely a documentation / discoverability
convenience; the same effect is obtained by calling
`MaskedLMMixin.compute_lm_loss(...)` directly.

members.summary: Base for all model-forward output dataclasses.
members.extended:
Provides a uniform protocol: outputs are simultaneously dataclass-like
(named fields, dict-style lookup) and sequence-like (iteration / tuple
indexing, with `None` fields silently elided).  Each concrete
subclass adds a task-specific set of `Tensor` (or
`tuple[Tensor, ...]`) fields.
examples:
>>> # ImageClassificationOutput is a typical subclass
>>> out = ImageClassificationOutput(logits=lucid.randn(1, 10))
>>> out["logits"].shape           # dict-style access
(1, 10)
>>> first, = out                  # tuple-style unpacking
>>> first is out.logits
True
notes:
Subclasses must be decorated with `@dataclass` so
`dataclasses.fields` works.  Iteration order follows field
declaration order, and `None` fields are skipped — this matches the
behaviour expected by callers that destructure outputs with
`logits, loss = model(x)`.

Direct instantiation of `ModelOutput` itself is not useful (no
fields) — always subclass and add `@dataclass`.

members.extended:
Currently identical in shape to the base protocol; the dedicated
type exists so static checkers can distinguish backbones from
task wrappers at call sites that demand one or the other.  A
feature-extraction utility, for example, should require a
backbone — not an `ImageClassification` head — to avoid
accidentally pulling logits.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import ResNet
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import BackboneProtocol, TaskWrapperProtocol
>>> isinstance(ResNet, BackboneProtocol)
True
>>> isinstance(ResNet, TaskWrapperProtocol)
True  # all backbones structurally satisfy the wrapper protocol too;
      # nominal naming is what distinguishes them
notes:
Naming rule: any public class in `_model.py` whose name lacks
the `For<CapWord>` pattern is treated as the backbone.  See
`arch-models-family-contract` slot 2.

members.name: HasConfigClass
members.summary:
Building-block protocol: model class points at its family's
Config via the `config_class` ClassVar.
members.extended:
Every concrete `PretrainedModel` subclass — backbone or
task wrapper — declares `config_class: ClassVar[type[...]] =
<FamilyConfig>`.  The base `PretrainedModel.__init__` uses the
attribute to validate the runtime config argument and
`from_pretrained` uses it to reconstruct the config from
JSON on disk.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import ResNet
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import HasConfigClass
>>> isinstance(ResNet, HasConfigClass)
True
>>> ResNet.config_class.__name__
'ResNetConfig'
notes:
Component of `PretrainedModelProtocol`.  Refine to
`BackboneProtocol` or `TaskWrapperProtocol` in
signatures that need finer distinctions.

attributes.name: config_class
attributes.description:
Pointer to the family's `ModelConfig` subclass.  Set
on every concrete model class; left as `None` on the
abstract `PretrainedModel` base so a missing override
raises a clear `TypeError` at first instantiation.

members.name: HasFamilyMeta
members.summary:
Building-block protocol: class has been decorated with
`@model_family_meta` and so carries the docs-facing metadata
triple at `__model_family_meta__`.
members.extended:
The decorator attaches a `ModelFamilyMeta` instance holding
`canonical_name` / `citation` / `theory` strings.  The docs
site reads these to render family-root index cards, sidebar
labels, and paper headers.  The Lucid contract requires every
concrete family Config to be wrapped.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import ViTConfig
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import HasFamilyMeta
>>> isinstance(ViTConfig, HasFamilyMeta)
True
A bare class without the decorator fails the check:
>>> class Bare: model_type = "bare"
>>> isinstance(Bare, HasFamilyMeta)
False
notes:
Component of `ModelConfigProtocol`.  Adding this protocol
to a function signature signals "must be a docs-discoverable
family Config" without committing to the rest of the contract
(registry id, dataclass-ness).

attributes.name: __model_family_meta__
attributes.description:
Frozen `lucid.models._meta.ModelFamilyMeta` instance set
by `@model_family_meta`.  The annotation is `object` here
because `Protocol` cannot import its concrete dataclass
without creating a circular dependency; use
`ModelFamilyMeta` in real signatures that need the typed
fields.

members.name: HasModelType
members.summary: Building-block protocol: class carries a `model_type` ClassVar.
members.extended:
The simplest of the family-contract pieces.  Every concrete family
Config (`ResNetConfig`, `BERTConfig`, …) overrides this ClassVar
to a unique short identifier (`"resnet"`, `"bert"`) used by the
registry, `config.json` persistence, and `AutoConfig` dispatch.

Intermediate abstract bases — `ModelConfig` itself,
`LanguageModelConfig`, `DiffusionModelConfig`,
`GenerativeModelConfig` — deliberately keep `model_type ==
"base"`; they are not family Configs and won't satisfy stricter
protocols (e.g. `ModelConfigProtocol`) that compose this
one.
examples:
Every documented family Config carries the attribute:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import ResNetConfig
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import HasModelType
>>> isinstance(ResNetConfig, HasModelType)
True
A plain class without the ClassVar does not satisfy it:
>>> class Plain: pass
>>> isinstance(Plain, HasModelType)
False
notes:
Component of the composite `ModelConfigProtocol`.  Use this
protocol alone in signatures that only need the family identifier
(e.g. logging utilities, registry lookups) without also requiring
the full `@model_family_meta` machinery.

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description:
Persistent family identifier — embedded in checkpoint
`config.json` and used by directory-based loading to dispatch
to the correct registry entry.

members.name: ModelConfigProtocol
members.summary: Composite structural contract for a family Config class.
members.extended:
A class satisfies this protocol when it simultaneously
1. declares a non-empty `model_type` ClassVar
   (`HasModelType`),
2. has been decorated with `@model_family_meta` so that
   `__model_family_meta__` is set (`HasFamilyMeta`),
3. is a `@dataclass` — the `__dataclass_fields__` ClassVar
   below is the witness that the dataclass transform ran.

Use this protocol in any function that accepts arbitrary family
Configs without coupling to the nominal `ModelConfig`
base — third-party model packages may satisfy it via duck
typing.
examples:
Every documented family Config satisfies the contract:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import ResNetConfig
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BERTConfig
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import ModelConfigProtocol
>>> all(isinstance(c, ModelConfigProtocol) for c in (ResNetConfig, BERTConfig))
True
A duck-typed third-party Config with the right shape also
qualifies — no need to inherit from ModelConfig:
>>> from typing import ClassVar
>>> class Stranger:
...     model_type: ClassVar[str] = "stranger"
...     __model_family_meta__: ClassVar[object] = object()
...     __dataclass_fields__: ClassVar[dict] = {}
>>> isinstance(Stranger, ModelConfigProtocol)
True
notes:
The protocol is `runtime_checkable`, so the static
validator (`tools/validate_model_zoo.py --runtime`) can use
`isinstance(cls, ModelConfigProtocol)` to catch refactor drift
(e.g. a decorator that silently strips
`__model_family_meta__`).  See `arch-models-family-contract`
for the full 5-slot family layout.

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description: Inherited from `HasModelType` — see that protocol.

attributes.name: __model_family_meta__
attributes.description: Inherited from `HasFamilyMeta` — see that protocol.

attributes.name: __dataclass_fields__

members.name: OutputDataclassProtocol
members.summary:
Forward-return dataclass shape — any dataclass whose name ends
with `Output` (e.g. `ImageClassificationOutput`,
`MaskedLMOutput`).  Carries at least an `logits` /
family-specific tensor field plus an optional `loss`.
members.extended:
The exact field set differs across tasks (logits / boxes / masks
/ samples / hidden_states / past_key_values / …), so the protocol
intentionally requires only the structural `__dataclass_fields__`
witness — no concrete field constraint.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models import ImageClassificationOutput
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import OutputDataclassProtocol
>>> isinstance(ImageClassificationOutput, OutputDataclassProtocol)
True
notes:
Output dataclasses are hidden from the docs sidebar
(`_model.py` 4-slot rule, slot 4) because they are *return
types* rather than user-facing entry points.  The protocol is
still useful for return-type hints in framework utilities (e.g.
metrics computation, loss aggregation) that accept any output
shape.

attributes.name: __dataclass_fields__
attributes.description: Standard attribute set by `dataclasses.dataclass`.

members.name: PretrainedModelProtocol
members.summary:
Composite structural contract for a family's *model* class —
either the backbone (`ResNet`, `BERTModel`) or a task wrapper
(`ResNetForImageClassification`, `BERTForMaskedLM`).
members.extended:
The protocol requires (i) the `config_class` pointer (from
`HasConfigClass`) and (ii) the two methods every
framework-consumed model exposes: `__init__(self, config)` and
`forward(...)`.  Concrete framework subclasses additionally
inherit `lucid.nn.Module` — so `parameters()` /
`state_dict()` / `train()` / `eval()` come for free — but
those are intentionally *not* part of the structural contract:
the protocol is meant to be checkable against the **class object
alone**, before instantiation, so the static validator can use
it.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import (
...     ResNet, ResNetForImageClassification,
... )
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import PretrainedModelProtocol
>>> isinstance(ResNet, PretrainedModelProtocol)
True
>>> isinstance(ResNetForImageClassification, PretrainedModelProtocol)
True
notes:
Refinements: `BackboneProtocol` for direct-model classes
(no `For<Task>` in name); `TaskWrapperProtocol` for
`*For<Task>` heads.  Use the refined protocol in signatures
that need to distinguish — e.g. a feature extractor utility
should take a `BackboneProtocol`, while a fine-tuning
helper might take a `TaskWrapperProtocol`.

attributes.name: config_class
attributes.description: Inherited from `HasConfigClass`.

members.name: TaskWrapperProtocol
members.summary:
Refinement of `PretrainedModelProtocol` for `*For<Task>`
head classes (e.g. `ResNetForImageClassification`,
`BERTForMaskedLM`).
members.extended:
Implementations are expected to return a task-specific `*Output`
dataclass from `forward` — `ImageClassificationOutput`
for classifiers, `MaskedLMOutput` for MLM heads, etc.  The
output type is not encoded in the protocol because Python's
structural typing cannot constrain method *return* shapes
without a Generic parameter, which would complicate the
runtime-checkable surface.
examples:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.resnet import ResNetForImageClassification
>>> from lucid.models._protocols import TaskWrapperProtocol
>>> isinstance(ResNetForImageClassification, TaskWrapperProtocol)
True
notes:
Naming rule: the class name ends with `For<CapWord>` matching
a key in `_TASK_SUFFIX_MAP` (see build script).  The validator
flags `For<Unknown>` suffixes with MZ050 so new task types
must be explicitly registered.

members.name: create_model
members.signature: create_model(name: str, pretrained: bool = False, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Look up `name` and call its factory — the timm-style entry point.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description:
Registered model name (case-insensitive, `-` / `_`
interchangeable).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Whether to download and load registered checkpoint weights.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Forwarded as keyword arguments to the underlying factory.  Most
factories pipe these into the `ModelConfig` constructor so
callers can adjust hyper-parameters at creation time without
defining a custom config (e.g. `num_classes=10` for transfer
learning).

raises.description: If `name` is not registered.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import create_model
>>> # CIFAR-10 transfer head — random init, 10 classes
>>> model = create_model("resnet_50", num_classes=10)
>>> model.config.num_classes
10
notes:
Equivalent to `model_entrypoint(name)(pretrained=pretrained,
**overrides)`.  For task-aware dispatch with the Auto* shells, see
`AutoModel` and the `AutoModelFor*` family.

members.name: is_model
members.summary: Return whether `name` is registered (case / separator insensitive).

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Candidate model name.

returns.description: `True` if a registry entry exists under the normalised name.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import is_model
>>> is_model("resnet_50")
True
>>> is_model("ResNet-50")
True
>>> is_model("not_a_real_model")
False

members.name: list_models
members.signature: list_models(task: str | None = None, family: str | None = None)
members.summary: List all registered model names, optionally filtered by task / family.

parameters.name: task
parameters.description:
Restrict to entries with this task tag (e.g.
`"image-classification"`).  `None` returns all tasks.

parameters.name: family
parameters.description:
Restrict to entries from this architecture family (e.g.
`"resnet"`).  `None` returns all families.

returns.description: Sorted list of normalised model names matching the filters.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import list_models
>>> list_models(family="resnet")[:3]
['resnet_101', 'resnet_152', 'resnet_18']
>>> "vit_base_16" in list_models(task="image-classification")
True
notes:
Use this for discovery — pair with `create_model` to build any
listed name.  The returned strings are the canonical (normalised)
forms; either `"resnet_50"` or `"ResNet-50"` is accepted as input
elsewhere, but the listing always returns the canonical spelling.

members.name: model_entrypoint
members.summary: Return the registered factory callable for `name`.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Registered model name (any case, hyphens or underscores).

raises.description:
If no entry is registered under the normalised name; the error
message includes up to three Levenshtein-near suggestions.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import model_entrypoint
>>> factory = model_entrypoint("resnet_50")
>>> model = factory(pretrained=False)
notes:
Task is **not** filtered here — `model_entrypoint` returns the
factory regardless of which Auto class it belongs to.  Use
`_registry_lookup` internally when you need task-filtered
dispatch.

members.name: register_model
members.signature: register_model(task: str = 'base', family: str | None = None, model_type: str | None = None, model_class: type[PretrainedModel] | None = None, default_config: ModelConfig | None = None, params: int | None = None, summary: object = 'auto')
members.summary: Decorator that registers a factory function under its `__name__`.
members.extended:
The global registry is the single source of truth for which models
Lucid exposes.  Every public factory in `lucid/models/**/_pretrained.py`
is decorated with `@register_model` so it becomes discoverable via
`create_model`, `list_models`, and the `AutoModelFor*`
family.

parameters.name: task
parameters.description:
Task tag used by Auto classes for typed dispatch.  Common values:
`"base"`, `"image-classification"`, `"object-detection"`,
`"semantic-segmentation"`, `"causal-lm"`, `"masked-lm"`,
`"seq2seq-lm"`, `"sequence-classification"`,
`"token-classification"`, `"question-answering"`,
`"image-generation"`.

parameters.name: family
parameters.description:
Architecture family identifier (e.g. `"resnet"`).  When `None`,
inferred from the factory's parent module name (the
second-to-last component of `fn.__module__`).

parameters.name: model_type
parameters.description:
Persistent `ModelConfig.model_type` value this factory produces.
Defaults to `family`.  Used by directory-based loading to match
on-disk `config.json` files against registered factories.

parameters.name: model_class
parameters.description:
The concrete `PretrainedModel` subclass returned by this
factory.  When supplied, directory-based loading
(`AutoModel.from_pretrained` against a path) avoids a
redundant factory call — strongly recommended.

parameters.name: default_config
parameters.description:
The default config produced when `pretrained=False`.  When
supplied, `AutoConfig.from_pretrained` returns it without
instantiating the model.

parameters.name: params
parameters.description:
Paper-cited trainable-parameter count for this factory's default
config (e.g. `61_100_840` for `alexnet_cls`).  Surfaced on
the API docs site as a `"61.1M"`-style tag on the factory
card and as a "Model Size" section on the detail page.  Omit
(`None`) when no authoritative count is available — the UI
then hides the tag rather than guessing.

parameters.name: summary
parameters.description:
Layer-tree summary used by the docs site's collapsible
"Model Size" section.  `"auto"` (the canonical value) tells
`tools/build_model_summaries.py` to introspect the model via
`shadow_alloc` and emit a real tree.  Pass an already-built
summary dict to override the introspection (rare — used only
when shadow-alloc introspection fails for a model family).

returns.description:
Decorator that returns the original factory unmodified (only the
registry side-effect occurs).

raises.description: If a model is already registered under the same normalised name.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models import register_model, PretrainedModel
>>> # Inside lucid/models/vision/myfamily/_pretrained.py:
>>> @register_model(
...     task="image-classification",
...     family="myfamily",
...     model_type="myfamily",
... )
... def myfamily_small(pretrained: bool = False, **overrides):
...     cfg = MyFamilyConfig(depth=12, **overrides)
...     return MyFamilyForImageClassification(cfg)
notes:
The decorator uses the factory's `__name__` (normalised through
`_normalize`) as the registry key.  Same name twice raises —
this catches accidental shadowing across families.
